speaker: interviewer
text: December 1, 1994 interview with Abraham Jachzel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jachzel, we already know and it has been established that you were at Mr. Schindler's factory. Tell me about any personal contact that you might have had with Mr. Schindler or his wife or his girlfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, his girlfriend, this was before. There wasn't his girlfriend, excuse me. This was Goeth's girlfriend. You know, I-- adopted dogs right away. My job, you know, to take care, you know. But how much could I take of the dogs? We put the steel and the chain on a pole. The dogs were so nervous and going around and around and around, they pull out-- if they pull out together getting with the-- with the-- with the ball. OK, we were in the clear. But sometimes, they hook up a certain way that it broke, you know were it starts, you know, where the point is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where he has to run, to go around. And-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose dogs were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose dogs were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the dogs. You know, Mr. [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, we-- they-- they were the masters and we were the dogs. [NON-ENGLISH] We were-- you know. Those were the dogs that were used for execution there, you know. Beautiful big dogs-- Doberman. There was one, I could never understand-- a Dalmatian, but a mixed Dalmatian with the Doberman, you know. I-- I had a Dalmatian here, you know. I couldn't teach her how to bark. And, and here, that Dalmatian was such a vicious dog, you know, one of the worst ones. So anyway, one time, they rip apart, and, and he, he comes down, and-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goeth. Goeth. And I was working. And they give me-- they give me a retard boy-- Pomeranz was his name-- to-- as my helper. So I'm taking him, you know, and I'm doing it. I'm showing, look, I got a ball bearing. He said, I don't care how much they're going to turn around, instead, the bearing is going to take the pressure, and it's going to take it off. He liked the idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, it was a crossbar, [POINTS] and I drilled out in the center. I put a bearing and the main screw. And with the chain, I said maybe this way you know, it's going to hold for a while. And I showed him and everything and I'm doing. And meantime, that girl, this-- his girlfriend, you know? Goeth's girlfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He-- he want to go down with a gun and finish us out. So she was holding him by the hand. I remember at that time, it was a beautiful day-- a summer, on a Sunday. And she said, no, [NON-ENGLISH], you know. Don't spoil our-- don't spoil our day, you know. This I remember exactly. You know, I could hear. There was like a little balcony there. And the dogs were, let's say, 30 feet away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, you know, when I take the toolbox and he takes the toolbox. And while I'm going backwards he says, stop. I said, that's it, you know? He shows me a crooked nail fell down. You know. I didn't pick up that nail. [NON-ENGLISH] So I picked up that nail and start yelling at my helper, you know, and cover him up. I didn't want-- you know, the idea is to cover him up, so he don't, don't do anything to him. But his girlfriend had the gun. You know? She, she didn't want to spoil that-- that beautiful day, that day when-- what is it-- this happened. So I, you know-- you know, she says it, you know, in the-- in the-- on television, there's everything. I said, look, whatever she was, whatever she was, my life-- she saved it. She saved your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would be-- I would be finished that day. And then you know, in, in Schindler's camp, when we were in Brunnlitz it was nice, you know. There was a soldier-- I don't know if it was a relative to Schindler, or to somebody. He came in. He was shot in the head. He had a concussion. And he used to stay by my leg all the time. It was very interesting, you know, it's very interesting the way it worked. And he wanted me to teach him. You know, he wanted to work. He brought me brass shells. He wanted I should make him a cigarette lighter, you know, from the brass shell. And I, I, I, I really taught him a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: He used to bring me two sandwiches every day. And this is the first time in my life-- it was chopped meat-- raw meat. And I wouldn't eat the raw, raw meat, you know. As hungry as I was, I'm very picky in certain things, you know? Maybe I'm not kosher, but I wouldn't take lobster or, or, or things, or whatever it is. Crustaceans.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't touch anything like that. This, this, this is a no-no. He brought me those two sandwiches. And my father, who was a big [NON-ENGLISH] you know, he got rid of that other half. I know I didn't. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, though, Mr. Schindler, was there ever personal contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Twice. Twice he came over. He sat. He'd stand by the machine and everything, and he liked it. And he asked me when those screws are going to be finished. And I know those screws are not for us. He-- he promised some other company, you know, he-- that he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I redid the work, then take 700 people, at least 500 of them doesn't know how a piece of steel looks like, you know. And we had to teach them. I didn't teach them, but there were people there to teach them. They teach them the heat treat, the presses. This is very easy, you know? And they'd set up a job. And we almost made the-- we made the finished product, but, up to-- up to the grinding. What do you think Mr. Schindler did for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What he did for me-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, thanks to him, I'm alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father. I don't think-- I could never make it-- Gross-Rosen, forget it. This-- I could never make it. Mauthausen-- for sure, I couldn't make it. So this is it, you know. Whatever he did-- I'm alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you left Mr. Schindler's factory and the Russians liberated you, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is most important. When we left, Schindler had a lot of materials. With that materials-- you know, silk, wool for suits, for coats for a shirt, for linen-- he had a lot of material. And before he left, he said, there's everything, give  everybody for two-- for a suit, for a coat, for six shirts, for so much underwear, you know? And they give us-- everybody had the same thing, certain amount. After it was finished, there was so much left that you could take as much as you can carry. Well, we couldn't carry any more. Well, you take 30, 40 pounds, you know, you had enough, you know. And believe me, when we came to Cracow, we already were rich. We already had something to sell. I remember I sold a piece of material. Right away, I had money-- right on the train before we even get to Cracow, I already had money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to Cracow to look for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to Cracow-- yes. And I was there with my father, and we went back to the house, and a friend of mine who was the housekeeper, the son, took us in. And then came in the lady downstairs. She needed something, some service, and this [INAUDIBLE] says, you know that lady lives in your apartment-- and he told her. So she starts-- oh, she started crying and everything. And she said, look, if you want it, I have so much space, you know. My husband, they took-- the Russians, and they never-- he never came back. He was director from the University Jagiellonski, you know. And she was all alone living in that big apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we going upstairs-- the paper, it's still there. The [POLISH] it was like a combination couch and bed together-- one piece. But I remember when I was a kid, you could lift it up and take out the beddings from, from inside, and pick up the headpiece. I had always apples there, or something, you know. When I went to sleep, I used to gnosh a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that [POLISH] is there. And she brings out blankets and she says, why don't you sleep here. Why-- why are you-- why are you going to-- this is the best place. So we already slept in our apartment. Yeah, I looked. There was the iron bed still. Nobody took it out from when my sister, little sister, was sleeping, you know. The white bed with brass--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We don't see those beds over there. Those are like a children bed. It wasn't a crib. It was bigger than a crib and smaller than a bed. You know? And it was still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't know it yet that maybe Adele is still alive, you know? And we go there, and the same noise, you know-- people go to the market. This is in the middle of the city and we sleep in our [POLISH]. It comes 11:00, 12:00, 1:00, 2:00-- we cannot fall asleep. So my father said, [SPEAKING POLISH]. In our bed. You know? His father said, we're no longer people. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: We can't sleep in beds. You know, it's, it's, it's-- we had to come-- take off the bedding, put it on the floor so we can sleep. And we fell asleep. [INTERPOSING VOICES] We were so de-humanized. This is my bed where I slept before the war, in the room. And then-- and then I come back, after the war, and I'm no more human. You know. So this was-- this was an experience. And the greatest thing what he did for us, what he-- we are alive. He gave us right away a [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: He gave him a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave us something to sell so when we came back-- it reminds you when the Jews came out from Egypt, [LAUGHS] they came away with the booty-- whatever, you know? But this was given to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened, about 6 days the friend of mine, [INAUDIBLE], say, you know what? They want you in the army. I was executive. In Europe, you're 21. You start to come-- say, say you are November-- and this was still, you know, three months before-- but they want you in the army. So I said oh yeah. Good. I said, [SPEAKING POLISH] I took the money and I went. And I went first of all to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left Cracow? I left Cracow. Went back to Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia, went back, went to Prague over there and I registered. And they give everybody a certain amount money. And they call it [POLISH], that was like the organization, maybe like [INAUDIBLE] or something like this. And they give us food, and they put in a beautiful hotel. Who, who did this for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Czechs.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Czechs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They put us up in a beautiful hotel. Matter of fact, the hotel had downstairs, had a theater and had a movies. It was very, very elegant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who funded this? Was it a Jewish organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- no. There was no Jewish organization. The Jewish organizations start-- 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this? This is '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is '45. This is '45. I go back there, and then there was a place that you register, they give you $15,000-- that money. It wasn't much. It was worth $5 maybe, or $4. And the food was good. The shows were for nothing. It was the first time I saw a hotel with four elevators. It was really-- you know, Prague is a-- it's a most beautiful city. It's much nicer than Cracow. It's not as old. And I was there. And then I went back. I went to Austria. There was a rabbi, a rabbi, a soldier-- an army rabbi. And he was taking those boys and bringing them to Austria. And from Austria, they were supposed to go to Italy, and from Italy to Israel-- to Palestine. It wasn't Israel. Anyway, I made the first step. I went to Austria. In Austria we were in a very beautiful city. And then comes a woman what I used to work for, her father-in-law Gemski, when I was studying here before, and his son was with us together. But he was put in jail. So I--

words.text: they were supposed to go to

speaker: interviewer
text: So she say, you know what? I think Adele is alive. But she was very sick. She-- she's sick on typhus, and she's in Bergen-Belsen. Well, you know what happened. I left everything, and I took off for Bergen-Belsen You left Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know whatever, whatever I had, you know-- the [NON-ENGLISH], all I did. And I give it to somebody else. I said, look, I want to travel light. I took some food only, and I-- and I traveled through Germany. Germany was smashed. There was no regular rail. I now have to zigzag like this, you know, to get-- until I get to Hanover. It took me, like, 8 or 9 days. And you had to cross Kassel. There was a train going, like squeaking, you know. You look down, you see ten stories, you know, down. You had to crawl two hours and look down, You know. Forget about it. Just to make that one bridge. If not, you would have to go another maybe 200 miles around.

speaker: interviewer
text: I made it. I come in. You know, I get into, to-- to-- with a train to [INAUDIBLE]. And there was trucks going into the camp, and I'm going to the camp. And then I see two girls holding my sister, you know. So all of a sudden, I said, what happened? She didn't get taller, but she looked taller, she got so skinny. She got thin hair on her head. The hair got so long. And skin and bone, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in, and I took over, and brought all the food. You know, there was a lot of food. [BACKGROUND NOISE] From the beginning, the English, they give them the wrong food, and a lot of people die from dysentery. But at that time, they already give him-- give crackers and give her soup. And, you know, and I fed her, fed her, fed her. And about four weeks later I said, listen, we're getting out from the camp. I said, now we can go to Sweden. I said, we're not going no place. I said, we got a father in Cracow. And we're going to go where people are. I'm not going any more in camps.

words.text: wrong food, and a lot of

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was somebody else that told me Stuttgart, there is is a city, like city camp. So they took off two streets, and they call it camp. And it's not a camp, you know, with the-- with the-- I say, I don't want to see any more soldiers. I don't want to see any guns. I want to live like everybody else, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very nice. I came to Stuttgart with her, and there was a-- they give us right away a room, you know. There was-- the apartments used to be three-bedroom apartments, most of it. That's how the Germans had it on that street. Fancy apartments. You know, it was a good section on top of the hill [INAUDIBLE]. And they gave us an apartment. And we stayed there and start-- I start looking around, you know, to make some money. As, as, as lucky as I was in ghetto and all over, I didn't have to say boo, and I was making money, coming in from all the sides, only to get beat up by my father. [SPEAKING POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you steal your money from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, I had to give my little sister, I remember, money. Say that you [POLISH] give it to you. [INTERPOSING VOICE]

speaker: interviewer
text: I knew they need it, you know? So from Stuttgart, you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Stuttgart, I stay there in Stuttgart. Then one day I said, I'm going to go back to Poland and make some money, bring some leather. You know, there was like 10 to 1. You spend $100 and you could bring it to Poland. And you came back, you could make, like, $1,000. I go in, they take away half of it. And the [POLISH] took the other half.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Pioneers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Pioneers, you know. And that's-- that, that Pioneer, the [INAUDIBLE], then I saw him here, you know. He was the [NON-ENGLISH] over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The head counselor.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] Kind of, you know, you're going to give me back those $300. Well, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister get to go see your father? Did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we find my father says he got married. I say, what the hell, Dad? Why did you need this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's only 35. Baloney. So I says to her, hey, look. Don't. You know we felt-- you know, we-- my sister is ready to get married. I'm a [NON-ENGLISH] you know, 20 to 22. He's getting married, you know. We felt very, very tight about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were sad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are we going to have little brothers, little sisters? It was sad. We didn't have the right. But anyway, that's how it is. Then I came to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: With what boat did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came with-- I started with the and the ship had the problems, you know, and the hurricane and everything. They came to pull us. And the other ship, the English ship who came to pull us, and broke in half. And, yeah. A ship-- we were going sideways, you know. All the motors went. We didn't have food. There was no light already at night on the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the ship was your father and your sister, and his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: On this ship that you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me. That's all. Me. Sleeping downstairs on a hammock. This was the liberty ships with the hammocks. And then you know, the water goes over the, the bow and everything. So at the end of it, there was a rabbi says, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was praying and I say, oy, you know. Anyway, it comes in the morning, the ocean became like a lake-- completely flat. And they came in. The captain didn't want to give up the ship anymore. And some mechanics went downstairs. I was already sick like a dog. And they fixed it, and we were running 6 knots an hour back to Plymouth-- Plymouth, England. And then, when we came in about 10, 15, miles before Plymouth, they wanted like, I don't know, [POLISH]. $3, $4 you know, pounds, for, for the parking, you know, for-- and the captain didn't want to give it to them. And then somehow, we had to be in the same ship. We were waiting until another ship came in who had to pick up other people from Germany. When they came back and picked us up, they were ferrying us from-- with a ferry boat, from one ship to another. And then it took another 10 days from Plymouth, England, to come to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it means we left March 10 and we came April 4, we came to United States. Tell me about your life today, Mr Jachzel. Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My life today?

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a wife. I have two children. I have two grandchildren. I was always a happy-go-lucky. I, I didn't get any breaks, you know? I never picked up an easy dollar, but I worked and I always-- I was comfortable. And I still am. I don't have big monies, or whatever it is, or big savings. But I am happy. I'm-- my kids don't need anything from me. They're very good situated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and when this happened to me, last week, in the Waldorf Astoria and being a guest of Steven Spielberg, it's still-- it's unbelievable in my head. I say, this was the pinnacle of my life, but I think this is it. I mean, what do I expect more? I think I, I, I-- He did what I couldn't do. When I came to United States and the cousins that came over, so they asked me how I-- I say look, this one is dead and that one is dead, and they're all dead. [SPEAKING POLISH] You know, nobody believes. And if I tell sometime, you know, a friend of mine comes in and we talk about the camp, you know. And my, my-- even my own kids, they say, so what else is new? You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, with the Schindler, and when we went to the movies and we saw it, I could see a spark of tear on my boy. I went special the second time. And I don't watch the movie, I watch his face straight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody believed you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to see it, or there is a little reaction. And I saw the reaction and I say, look, he did what I couldn't do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Spielberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Spielberg put it in. And every time I came to Poldek Pfefferberg, to Los Angeles, hey, what's going on? You know. When it's going to be? When it's going to be, that movie? What's going to  happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, he's asking me question, and he thinks the other way. I think nothing's going to happen from it, you know? Or I'm crazy or he's crazy. That's what I heard from Poldek, OK? So looks like he wants to be a genius and he thinks different way. You know, a producer, or whatever it is that-- they're special people, they see it in a different way. And believe me, he did such a nice way-- not to hurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you know, and-- you know, I really, I really think you, you couldn't do any better. If you were to show a little more, you couldn't sell the ticket. Because it would be too sad?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be too sad. They couldn't handle. I couldn't handle, if I had to see it. You know, like going to my own funeral, you know. But this way, it was a little humane, it is.

words.text: going to my own funeral, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jachzel, our time is almost up, I want to ask you one question. It's 50 years after the war on Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message, if any, would like to leave to your children, to your grandchildren, to your great-grandchildren, to the future about what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep on. Do your best. Be humane. Be nice to your friends, and be nice to everybody. Love your neighbor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Love your children. Love you. Have respect for people. That's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You can go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? Well, on the left, you have my grandmother and my aunt in the middle, sending off her son in the First World War to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what are their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is [INAUDIBLE]. The other is Sara, and Uncle Carmen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me that your father is on the right hand side with the mustache.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the front row.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the center is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi Schneerson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi, great Rabbi. This is in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what year approximately?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1945. Hold on one moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, tell me the name of the previous-- the previous photo was-- The previous photo was in honor of the great Rabbi Lubavitcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lubavitcher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which in center of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, now tell me this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this photo is a sendoff 1934, maybe '33. This is the-- my whole family, [INAUDIBLE] all cousins and uncles and-- sending off-- her name was [? Pepka-- ?] to Israel. They brought over-- the men-- the men came from Israel. From-- at that time, it was Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someone in the back there. That's where about.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the back corner on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not sure which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure if it's me or who-- or is the other cousin of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and tell me where this is and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [INAUDIBLE] you know, in [INAUDIBLE] in Cracow. This was before the war, like a pilgrimage. Like people go to Jerusalem, people from all over Europe used to come in Lag BaOmer. This was his yahrzeit. And today, all you see is a handful of people on his yahrzeit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1945. Lag BaOmer was right at the war. It comes up about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are, are you in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father is here with the delegation of the-- whatever the head of the Jewish community in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1945, about three months after the war. This is the picture, the first hair what I could comb. [INAUDIBLE] first haircut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stuttgart.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me who this is and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is right after the war. My father married that woman. This is still before [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your father's full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Solomon Jachzel. I mean, Jachzel it would be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malka.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her--

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, her name was still Malka Zukerman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where was this taken and what--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Cracow about-- in about August in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1945, OK. Just one moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This one. This is my sister. It's about 1946, and-- made in Stuttgart, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me your sister's full name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Adele Jachzel, now it's Adelle [? Blumenstyk. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you can. Go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this happened in about 1945 or-- or maybe beginning of '46. We got impatient. We wanted to go to Palestine at that time. So we set up a protest. And it was very noisy in Stuttgart that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had some kind of promise from General Clay, I think it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell me. Then where-- what sort of-- OK. Are you in the center? You're in the center with the large, white lapels. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm with the navy jacket and-- right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Great. Thank you. Tell me, who were the-- who organized-- helped organize that kind of a protest?

speaker: interviewee
text: That kind of protest, it got organized-- I think it was by us, and maybe by-- maybe most of it by us. We were disgusted. After a year waiting, and we didn't know where we were going to go. Nobody was talking about going to the United States. We knew that we cannot go back to Poland. So we started to make noise-- send us to Palestine, that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: This, you said-- wait, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, wait. Wait. All right. This is in Stuttgart on a [POLISH] around that section. And I'm sitting here waiting for-- I had a-- I had to wait for somebody, had to bring something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1946 or-- If it's winter, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, my sister, and my brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, on the left is you.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is me, my sister in the middle, and my future brother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Victor [? Blumenstyk. ?] She's his wife now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in 1947, March 10. --tomorrow?

speaker: interviewer
text: This one now.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's about the first picture I did in the United States. I had to send one to Stuttgart, to my sister, and one to Poland, to Cracow, to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me with my two children, Steve and--
